coredata.dc:title: Remaining useful life prediction for lithium-ion battery by combining an improved particle filter with sliding-window gray model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lin
An, Jingjing
Wang, Huimin
Zhang, Mo
Pan, Haihong
coredata.dc:description: Dependable and accurate battery remaining useful life (RUL) prediction is essential for ensuring the safety and reliability of battery systems. To improve the dynamic traceability of the battery degradation process for RUL prediction under different loading profiles, this paper presents an improved RUL prediction method, which is established from the combination of the linear optimization resampling particle filter (LORPF) with the sliding-window gray model (SGM). Major innovations are presented as follows: (1) To increase the accuracy of RUL prediction, a linear optimization combination is proposed to overcome the particle diversity deficiency in the resampling process of the standard PF, i.e. the LORPF; (2) To improve the traceability of the LORPF in predicting degradation trajectory, the SGM is employed to update the state variables of the state–space model in the LORPF. Additionally, an SGM-LORPF framework is constructed for RUL prediction. The performance of the SGM-LORPF is synthetically verified by data from two types of batteries under different loading profiles. Prediction test results indicate that the SGM-LORPF can achieve accurate RUL prediction under both constant current discharge conditions (relative error within 7.20%) and dynamic current discharge conditions (relative error within 2.75%). Moreover, using only a small amount of historical data, the proposed SGM-LORPF framework can acquire accurate results. The experimental outcome indicates that the SGM-LORPF has considerable efficiency and a wide range of practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Remaining useful life
Sliding-window gray model
Particle filter
Linear optimization resampling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RUL prediction framework based on SGM-LORPF algorithm.
The capacity prediction results of B0005 and S43 by SGM.
RUL prediction results of three methods for B0005 and S43.
Capacity prediction results at different prediction starting points.
Accuracy analysis of RUL prediction results.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
* RUL
T
is the real RUL and RUL
P
is the predicted RUL.

head.ce:title: Remaining useful life prediction for lithium-ion battery by combining an improved particle filter with sliding-window gray model

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechatronics Engineering, College of Mechanical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechatronics Engineering, College of Mechanical Engineering, Guangxi University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center of Renewable Energy Materials (CICREM), Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center of Renewable Energy Materials (CICREM), Guangxi University, Nanning, 530000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No. 100 Daxue Road, Xixiangtang District, Nanning City, Guangxi Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dependable and accurate battery remaining useful life (RUL) prediction is essential for ensuring the safety and reliability of battery systems. To improve the dynamic traceability of the battery degradation process for RUL prediction under different loading profiles, this paper presents an improved RUL prediction method, which is established from the combination of the linear optimization resampling particle filter (LORPF) with the sliding-window gray model (SGM). Major innovations are presented as follows: (1) To increase the accuracy of RUL prediction, a linear optimization combination is proposed to overcome the particle diversity deficiency in the resampling process of the standard PF, i.e. the LORPF; (2) To improve the traceability of the LORPF in predicting degradation trajectory, the SGM is employed to update the state variables of the state–space model in the LORPF. Additionally, an SGM-LORPF framework is constructed for RUL prediction. The performance of the SGM-LORPF is synthetically verified by data from two types of batteries under different loading profiles. Prediction test results indicate that the SGM-LORPF can achieve accurate RUL prediction under both constant current discharge conditions (relative error within 7.20%) and dynamic current discharge conditions (relative error within 2.75%). Moreover, using only a small amount of historical data, the proposed SGM-LORPF framework can acquire accurate results. The experimental outcome indicates that the SGM-LORPF has considerable efficiency and a wide range of practicality.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on energy-saving and low-emission electric vehicles (EVs), driven by the growth of concern on the global energy crisis and environmental pollution, has become a vital issue in automobile industry. Recently, lithium-ion batteries are widely used as power sources in EVs, attributed to advantages such as high energy density, low self-discharge rate, no memory effect, and so forth (Xiao, 2015; Wei et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2015; He et al., 2013; Williard et al., 2013). During a battery’s life span, its operating performance, stability, and safety will deteriorate with the increase of cycles. To assess the remaining cycles of a battery in advance, numerous studies have been carried out to explore battery remaining useful life (RUL) prediction. RUL can be defined as the remaining useful life on an asset at a particular time of operation (Si et al., 2011). An accurate RUL prediction can provide a valuable reference for battery maintenance and replacement (Dong et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2013; Eddahech et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2016).

In existing literature, the RUL prediction method can be generally categorized into the model-based method, the data-driven method, and the hybrid method (Lipu et al., 2018; GuoZ. Li and Pecht, 2015). The model-based method can be further divided into mechanism-based method and empirical-based method. Eddahech et al. (2012) analyzed batteries using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to obtain the internal resistance at low frequency and predicted the battery RUL according to the relationship between internal resistance and the number of cycles. However, the EIS test is highly demanding on equipment and takes a long time, which is not conducive to online implementation. To obtain the EIS data online, Guha and Patra (2018) developed a fractional-order equivalent circuit to evaluate EIS, and the evaluated EIS was used to build a regression model for battery RUL prediction. Although the mechanism-based method typically has high accuracy, it is difficult to apply it to other types of batteries when a particular type of battery is modeled. By contrast, the empirical-based method is flexible to build and applicable to a wide range of scenarios. Saha et al. (2009) asserted that the battery capacity tends to exhibit exponential decay with an increase of the number of cycles. To fit the capacity degradation curves, Xing et al. (2013) developed an ensemble model by fusing an exponential model with a polynomial model, utilizing a particle filter (PF) to adjust the ensemble model parameters and estimate battery capacity, and predicting the RUL by extrapolating the ensemble model to the battery end of life. By comparing the results of ensemble model to the individual results of the exponential model and polynomial model, they found that the ensemble model showed better prediction performance. In general, the empirical-based method requires a lot of data for curve fitting; therefore, its prediction accuracy is unsatisfactory when historical data is insufficient or poor in quality.

With the rapid evolution of machine learning technology, various data-driven methods for battery RUL prediction have been developed, such as the unscented Kalman filter (UKF), deep neural networks (DNN), relevance vector machine (RVM), particle filter (PF), and so forth. Walker et al. (2015) used three algorithms to predict RUL with the same battery degradation model, and the resulting comparison among the methods indicated that the PF algorithm is more accurate than non-linear least squares and UKF, but its computational cost is greater than the other two algorithms. To downsize the cost of calculation and improve the accuracy of RUL prediction, Zheng and Fang (2015) employed a relevance vector regression model to predict UKF future residuals, and used the resulting future residuals to recursively estimate the battery degradation model parameters for RUL prediction. Considering the convenience of data acquisition, Wu et al. (2016) applied the terminal voltage curve during the charge process to reflect battery RUL, discerned the relationship between RUL and terminal voltage curve utilizing a feed forward neural network (FFNN), and employed an importance sampling for FFNN input selection. Ren et al. (2018) developed an autoencoder to extract a multi-dimensional feature for building a battery degradation model to more adequately reflect the battery RUL, and used the degradation model trained by DNN to predict battery RUL. Overall, these data-driven methods do not need to understand the electrochemical mechanism inside the battery because they are effective at mapping the relationships between accessible data and hidden information. However, when the amount of data is not sufficient enough, it is difficult to guarantee high prediction accuracy of the entire battery life. In addition, powerful hardware is required to conduct the RUL prediction by these methods in a practical battery management system.

Obviously, the model-based method and data-driven method have their own individual limitations in battery RUL prediction. In light of this consideration, the hybrid method was proposed to further improve the RUL prediction accuracy by integrating the advantages of the model-based method and the data-driven method. Zhang et al. (2018) used the UKF to generate an importance function for improving the PF algorithm, and subsequently, the improved PF was combined with an exponential model to predict battery RUL. Wang et al. (2016) considered the presence of additive Gaussian noises in the RUL prediction process to reduce the complexity of RUL prediction method and ensure the prediction accuracy, and thus proposed a sigma-point Kalman filter to update the empirical model parameters for RUL prediction, which revealed that the sigma-point Kalman filter required less computational effort than PF algorithm. Hu et al. (2018) employed a sparse Bayesian learning module to infer the battery capacity from charge-related features and a recursive Bayesian filtering module to update the capacity fade model parameters for assessing battery RUL. In these articles, the objective of hybrid methods aimed to promote the accuracy of the battery RUL prediction by combining the benefits of the model-based methods with those of the data-driven methods. Unfortunately, hybrid methods are usually accompanied by a high computational cost, and they are only able to achieve satisfactory results for short-term prognostics. When the prediction horizon becomes longer, the prediction performance of hybrid methods will decline to battery degradation uncertainty.

As noted earlier, these RUL prediction methods have their own merits and demerits, and there is still margin for improvement. In this paper, a novel hybrid method based on the sliding-window gray model (SGM) and the linear optimization resampling particle filter (LORPF), i.e. the SGM-LORPF framework, was proposed to overcome the following limitations and dilemmas: (1) accurate long-term prediction depends on a sufficient amount of data, and (2) the resampling process of standard PF will lead to the loss of particle diversity. A key contribution of this study is the SGM, developed to predict battery capacity. To improve prediction accuracy, SGM uses a sliding-window mechanism to dynamically update the model parameters based on the concept of completely utilizing new information. Another contribution is the SGM was integrated with an improved PF for accurate RUL prediction. To enhance the diversity of particles in PF algorithm, a linear optimization combination was introduced into the resampling process. The development coefficients of SGM were used to derive the measurement function of LORPF, and an exponential growth model was utilized to build the state transition function of LORPF for tracking capacity degradation. The gray system theory is well-known for modeling with only a limited amount of data to predict the dynamic behavior of the concerning system. Therefore, the proposed SGM-LORPF framework can be used for RUL prediction with a small amount of data. The test datasets from NASA’s Ames Research Center were incorporated and a self-built experimental platform was established to verify the efficacy of the proposed SGM-LORPF framework.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the experimental setup to acquire the battery degradation datasets. Section 3 provides the detailed implementations of the SGM-LORPF framework for RUL prediction. Section 4 analyzes the feasibility of the proposed method and presents some indicators to evaluate the RUL prediction results. Conclusions and future works are summarized in Section 5.

The experimental platform is shown in Fig. 1. It includes a thermal chamber for maintaining the environment, a DC power supply (ITECH IT6523D) for charging the battery, a DC electronic load (ITECH IT8816B) for generating loading profiles, and a host computer for controlling the experiment online and recording data. The Samsung ICR18650-26F (LiCoO2) lithium-ion batteries with a graphite anode are used in the test, and their main specifications are demonstrated in Table 1. During the charging/discharging processes, the current, voltage, and temperature of each battery are measured and recorded at 1 Hz.

Two batteries (S42 and S43) from the same batch were selected for experimentation in this study and placed in battery holders in a thermal chamber. The test schedule shown in Fig. 2 was implemented as follows. (1) A characteristic test was carried out at temperature
T = 10
°C, followed by two identical tests conducted at
T = 25
°C and
T = 40
°C. In each characteristic test, a capacity test was conducted to measure the battery’s maximum usable capacity. Next, a new European driving cycle (NEDC) test, an urban dynamometer driving schedule (UDDS) test, and a Japanese 1015 mode driving cycle (JP1015) test were consecutively conducted to stimulate batteries in driving cycle-based conditions. Between two adjacent test points in each characteristic test, batteries were charged by a constant current and a constant voltage (CC–CV)​ strategy. (2) The aging test was conducted at 45 °C. The batteries were charged by a CC–CV​ strategy, and they were discharged by a hybrid loading profile test (combining NEDC, UDDS, and JP1015). In each cycle, the batteries were continually discharged until they reached the lower cut-off voltage. The entire test schedule was repeated until the battery capacity degraded to 2.08 Ah (80% of nominal capacity).

After running the experiment with the battery schedule, this paper will introduce a novel RUL prediction framework in this section. To begin, the study proposes the LORPF algorithm by improving the resampling process of standard PF and uses a sliding-window mechanism to establish an SGM for enhancing the long-term prediction capability of the gray model. Next, an SGM-LORPF framework can be constructed by combining the LORPF algorithm and the SGM to predict battery RUL.

In a standard PF algorithm, the introduction of the resampling process can eliminate the particle degeneracy phenomenon during filtering, but the process will also lead to the loss of particle diversity. As a result, the loss of particle diversity will influence the filtering performance and even the filtering diffusion. To increase the particle diversity and improve the precision of the standard PF algorithm, the study developed the linear optimization resampling particle filter (LORPF) algorithm. Generally, many dynamic systems can be described by a dynamic state–space model, which consists of a state transition function Eq. (1)(a) and a measurement function Eq. (1)(b). Consider a dynamic system model as follows:
(1)

x
k
represents the state variable of the system at time k ;
y
k
represents the measured value of the system at time k ; and
v

are the process and measurement noises. They are mutually independent, and the covariance of them are
Q

(4.2) The random resampling method of the standard PF is used to divide the sampled particles
x

into two groups (Gordon et al., 1993). Particles with greater weights are placed in a copy group, and particles with smaller weights should be placed in an abandon group.

(4.3) The abandon group is further divided into a pseudo-abandon group and a complete abandon group. If the weight of a particle in the abandon group is larger than the threshold
ω
t h r e s
as calculated by Eq. (5), then the particle should be kept in the pseudo-abandon group waiting for linear combination. Otherwise, this particle will be completely abandoned.

In addition to improving the resampling process, it is also important to build an efficient dynamic system model for enhancing the prediction accuracy of the LORPF. Herein, the SGM, which can characterize the complex degradation trend by dynamically updating model parameters, is introduced to establish the measurement function of the LORPF and produce the SGM-LORPF framework.

GM (1, 1) is one of the most widely-used gray models and its detailed implementation process has been outlined in our previous studies (Chen et al., 2018). In GM (1, 1), known historical data is used for modeling in its entirety. However, the SGM only utilizes recent data to update model parameters and increases the prediction accuracy in long-term prediction. The schematic diagram of the SGM is displayed in Fig. 3, and its specific steps are as follows:

Step 1: Suppose that
{
y
1
,
y
2
,
… ,
y
n
}
represents the entire known historical capacity data, and
y
n
is the battery capacity at cycle n; then, an original sequence
{
y
n − m + 1
,
y
n − m + 2
,
… ,
y
n
}
can be selected according to the window size m.

Step 2: Input the original sequence
{
y
n − m + 1
,
y
n − m + 2
,
… ,
y
n
}
into GM (1, 1), and the GM (1, 1) will output the predictive capacity
y
ˆ
n + 1
at cycle n+1. In this process, the original sequence
{
y
n − m + 1
,
y
n − m + 2
,
… ,
y
n
}
is first used to calculate the development coefficient
a
1
, and then the development coefficient
a
1
is used to obtain the predictive capacity
y
ˆ
n + 1
as shown in Eq. (10), which is the recursive form of GM (1, 1).

y

in the original sequence

y

n
}

ˆ
n + 1

{

}
into GM (1, 1).
Step 4: Repeat step 3 until the presupposed battery capacity failure threshold is met.

Then the predicted capacity results calculated by the SGM are used as the measurement values of the LORPF to construct an SGM-LORPF framework. In other words, the recursive form of GM (Eq. (10)) is used to predict and replace the measured value
y
k
in the measurement function (Eq. (1)(b)), and the measurement noise
w
k
is added to represent the influence of noise

where

is the battery capacity at cycle k.

After establishing the measurement function Eq. (11) in Section 3.2, we also need to build a specific state transition function of the LORPF for accurately predicting RUL. Battery capacity decreases exponentially with the increase of number of cycles. An exponential growth model is built to describe the capacity degradation trend of batteries, as shown in Eq. (12)
(12)
x
k
=
β
1
exp
(
β
2
⋅ k )
+
β

(

x
k
is the battery capacity at cycle k;
β
1
,
β
2
,
β
3
and
β
4
are the model parameters.

The exponential growth model is not only the battery aging model, but also the state transition function of the LORPF. Consequently, based on Eq. (12), the state transition function Eq. (1)(a) can be revised as follows:
(13)

k

β

represents the process noise.
The specific implementation process of the RUL prediction framework is shown in Fig. 4. The RUL prediction task can be primarily summarized in the four steps below:

Step 1: Chose a degradation dataset

C

In this section, capacity datasets of S43 discharged with dynamic loading profiles and B0005 from NASA’s datasets (constant-current discharging) were used to analyze the effectiveness of capacity prediction using the SGM and the SGM-LORPF. The capacity failure thresholds of B0005 and S43 were set to 1.4Ah (70% of nominal capacity) and 2.08Ah (80% of nominal capacity), respectively. Three methods, including the standard PF, LORPF, and SGM-LORPF were carried out for RUL prediction and analyzed at different starting points. The prediction starting points were set as 1/3, 1/2, and 2/3 of whole life cycles (WLC).

There are obvious differences between the capacity degradation trajectories of B0005 (Fig. 5 a–c) and S43 (Fig. 5d–f) because of different aging tests. The capacity trajectory of B0005 fluctuates frequently and there are many capacity regenerations in the degradation process. In comparison, the SGM can still accurately track the change of capacity and simulate fluctuations. In Fig. 5(a) and (c), there are capacity fluctuations close to the prediction starting points (1/3 and 2/3 WLC), and modeling the SGM with these capacity data will affect the initial prediction results, which leads to some deviations between the predicted capacity and the real capacity in the initial stage. The sliding window mechanism can effectively use the new data to update model parameters and reflect the latest changes in the trend of capacity. Thus, the predicted capacity is gradually converging to the real capacity. In Fig. 5(b), the predicted capacity trajectory is highly consistent with the real capacity because the RUL prediction of B0005 starts from a relatively smooth (1/2 WLC) stage of capacity change.

The real capacity trajectory of S43 is roughly smooth. There are only a few inflexion points where the slope of the degradation trajectory changes is pronounced. For the RUL prediction, when the starting point of S43 is close to the capacity inflection point (Fig. 5d), the trend of predicted capacity is similar to the real capacity but there is a clear deviation between them because the modeling data of the SGM are placed before the inflection point, which causes the SGM to inaccurately predict the trend of subsequent capacity. However, the predicted trajectory can still change correspondingly with the real trajectory. For Fig. 5(e) and (f), the predicted capacity is consistent with the real capacity, although it deviates at the end of the capacity degradation trajectory Because the capacity degrades faster and the prediction by the SGM has not fully converged.

The maximum error (max error), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and standard deviation (STD) are applied to quantitatively assess the capacity prediction results in Table 2. The capacity prediction errors are gradually decreasing as the starting points of prediction are increasing. Nevertheless, the characteristics of error change are distinctly different between B0005 and S43. The max error of B0005 is relatively small, but the MAPE and the STD indicating global error are relatively large, especially when the prediction starting point is the 80th cycle (Fig. 5c), when the MAPE reaches 0.0195. The aging process of S43 was accelerated by the complex dynamic discharge profiles, and its max errors are relatively larger than those of B0005 with different starting points. Nonetheless, the max errors of the two batteries are both less than 0.12Ah. The results in Fig. 5 and Table 2 synthetically indicate that the SGM can track and predict the degradation of battery capacity under constant-current discharging and dynamic loading profiles. The SGM will be used as the capacity degradation model for the subsequent RUL prediction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RUL Prediction with the SGM-LORP for discharge condition
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed SGM-LORPF is compared with the standard PF and the LORPF to verify its performance and applicability. The initialization parameters for the three methods are set as follows: the initial number of particles
N = 500
, the covariance of process noise
R
v
= 0 . 0001
, and the covariance of measurement noise
R
w
= 0 . 0001
. The parameters of the SGM are the same as those in Section 4.1. The RUL prediction results with three methods for two types of batteries are displayed in Fig. 6.

The performance of the SGM-LORPF excels in degradation trajectory tracking and RUL prediction over the PF and LORPF methods. The predicted trajectory by the SGM-LORPF deviates from the real data at the beginning of prediction in Fig. 6(a), but it gradually converges to the real data as the cycle number increases. In Fig. 6(b) and (c), the prediction trajectories of B0005 using the SGM-LORPF almost coincide with real trajectories when the prediction starts from the 1/2 and 2/3 WLC values. Furthermore, the range of PDF of the SGM-LORPF is the narrowest, which indicates the high probability that prediction results fall in the given range and the prediction is extremely reliable. Predicted trajectories of the PF method have significant deviations from the real data, especially when the prediction starting points are approximately 1/3 and 1/2 WLC (Fig. 6 a and b). Although the prediction trajectories of the PF are shown to be converging to the real data with increasing cycle numbers, their accuracy and stability are not comparable to those of the SGM-LORPF. The prediction results of the LORPF method are similar to the PF, which are relatively smooth and solely exhibit the general trend of capacity degradation. This indicates the SGM utilized to construct the measurement function of the SGM-LORPF can effectively improve the traceability in RUL prediction.

The degradation trajectory of S43 is relatively smooth, thus the predicted trajectories of the three methods are all in line with the real trajectory. The prediction results of the SGM-LORPF are closer to the real RUL than those of the PF and the LORPF (Fig. 6d–f). Predicted results of the PF deviate greatly from the real RUL once the violent fluctuation occurs near the prediction starting point (Fig. 6d); on the other hand, the LORPF and the SGM-LORPF still achieved accurate prediction results. This means that linear optimization resampling can significantly improve the traceability of the PF by enhancing the diversity of particles in the resampling process of the standard PF. When the prediction starting points are set to 1/2 WLC and 2/3 WLC (Fig. 6 e and f), the RUL prediction accuracy of the three different methods is obviously higher than that in Fig. 6(d). The more historical data are used for modeling, the higher accuracy can be achieved in model fitting and algorithm tracking for the PF and the LORPF. Prediction results of the SGM-LORPF are much closer to the real values than those of the PF and the LORPF in Fig. 6(e), and their accuracy is significantly higher due to the ability of the model to update values in the sliding-window mechanism. Moreover, PDFs of the SGM-LORPF are the narrowest among three methods as shown in Fig. 6(e) and Fig. 6(f), indicating that the results of the SGM-LORPF have smaller dynamic range than that of the other two methods. Overall, the SGM-LORPF demonstrates the best performance in both accuracy and stability for RUL prediction out of all three models.

The absolute error (AE), root mean square error (RMSE), and relative error (RE) (Chen et al., 2020) are used as criteria to quantitatively evaluate the accuracy of prediction results for the three methods (Table 3). When the RUL is predicted from 1/3 WLC, the prediction errors of the two batteries using the LORPF and the SGM-LORPF are much smaller than those of the PF, because the linear optimization resampling technique effectively avoids the issue of particle diversity loss for the standard PF and consequently improves the RUL prediction accuracy. The AEs of the SGM-LORPF are less than 10 cycles except that of the S43 at the prediction starting point of 1/3 WLC, which means the prediction results of the SGM-LORPF have both better accuracy and robustness compared to the other two algorithms.

The results of the SGM-LORPF in Fig. 6 and Table 3 are significantly better than those of the LORPF because the SGM-LORPF regards the predicted capacity value of the SGM as the measurement value of the LORPF, and the ability of the SGM in exploring the trend of capacity degradation is fully utilized. With increased historical data are used for modeling, the AE and RE values of the three algorithms decrease gradually, although the SGM-LORPF illustrates superiority in degradation trajectory fitting and prediction accuracy over the PF and the LORPF. More historical data are used for the establishment of an accurate model, which is conducive to improve the accuracy of RUL prediction. For the SGM-LORPF framework, errors are mainly derived from the cumulative process of repeated modeling in the long-term prediction. But these cumulative errors are diminished by the model-updating mechanism of the SGM, and the accuracy of the SGM-LORPF is significantly better than that of the PF and the LORPF in long-term RUL prediction. As such, the SGM-LORPF has a wide range of practicality and universality.

A synergy of the SGM model with an improved PF has been integrated to synthesize an SGM-LORPF framework for RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries. The primary conclusions of this study are summarized as follows:
(1) A battery test setup was designed and several related experiments were performed, including the measurement of real capacity and tests of dynamic loading profiles at different temperatures.

(2) The linear optimization resampling technique improved the resampling process of the standard PF. A comparison of the RUL prediction results indicated that the LORPF has higher accuracy than the standard PF.

(3) The SGM-LORPF further improved the prediction performance of the LORPF by revising the measurement function of the LORPF with the SGM. Two types of batteries were used to validate the effectiveness of the SGM-LORPF framework, further advocating that the SGM-LORPF produces better RUL prediction results than those of the standard PF and the LORPF.

In practical applications, the ambient temperature varies dynamically throughout the batteries’ life-time, and the loading profiles are much more complex than those simulated the experiments. In the future, more experiments will be conducted under changing ambient temperatures to further explore the impacts of temperature on the RUL prediction. Moreover, future studies will expand the implementation of the SGM-LORPF in more working conditions, including different depth-of-discharge and energy recovery. More improvements for the SGM-LORPF will be proposed to provide a synthetic way for better RUL prediction accuracy.

Lin Chen: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Jingjing An: Software, Writing - original draft. Huimin Wang: Methodology, Validation, Data curation. Mo Zhang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Haihong Pan: Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China(Grant No. 51667006).
